title: SEGMENT 3 — The development pipeline — concept to approval, end to end

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The development pipeline — concept to approval, end to end
Let me draw the pipeline for you.
I want you to be able to sketch this on a napkin by the end of this segment.
You start, at the leftmost end of the pipeline, with a concept.

Somebody — usually an academic researcher, sometimes a company scientist — has the idea that a particular disease could be treated by delivering a particular gene, or by editing a particular target, in a particular tissue.

The idea might come from a study showing that loss of a gene causes a disease, or that overexpression of a gene rescues a phenotype in a mouse, or that a particular pathway is dysregulated in a tumor.
The concept stage is, in regulatory terms, completely unregulated.
Anybody can have an idea.
The agency does not get involved until you start to take real steps toward making the idea into a drug.
The next box on the pipeline is preclinical development.
This is the stage at which you start doing the actual experiments — first in cell culture, then in animal models — that would justify dosing the therapy into a human being.
We will spend an entire segment on what the preclinical package for a gene therapy looks like, because it has specific requirements that differ from those for small molecules.

For now, hold in your head that preclinical is the bench-and-animal stage, it lasts typically two to five years for a gene therapy, and it is where the majority of gene therapy programs die without ever reaching a human.
If preclinical work is going well and you are starting to imagine a clinical trial, you enter the pre-IND stage.
This is the period during which you start having formal conversations with FDA about your program — meetings called Type B or Type C meetings, which we will discuss in detail in Segment 5.
The pre-IND phase is, in a sense, the "are we on the right path" check.
You go to the agency with your preclinical data, your manufacturing plan, your proposed trial design, and you ask them: does this look like something we could file an IND on?
The agency gives feedback.
You go back, do more work, address the feedback, and eventually you decide you are ready to file.
The next box is the IND — the Investigational New Drug application.
This is the document — actually a set of documents, often running to thousands of pages — that you submit to FDA to ask for permission to begin human trials.
The IND is, in a sense, the first regulatory checkpoint that has real teeth.
You submit it, and you have to wait thirty days.
If the agency does not put you on a clinical hold during that thirty-day window, you can begin dosing patients.
If they do put you on hold, you cannot start until you have addressed their concerns and they have lifted the hold.
We will spend a full segment on the IND because it is the most important single document in the early life of a drug.
If the IND clears, you enter the clinical phase, which traditionally has three sub-stages: Phase 1, Phase 2, and Phase 3.
Phase 1 is the first time the drug is given to humans, usually a small number — twenty to a hundred — and the primary question is safety.
Phase 2 expands the population, typically several hundred patients, and starts to ask whether the drug actually works.
Phase 3 is the big pivotal trial — often hundreds to thousands of patients — designed to definitively demonstrate efficacy and safety in a population that approximates the eventual market.

For gene therapy, as we will see, the boundaries between these phases are often blurred — Phase 1 and Phase 2 are frequently combined into a single trial, because the patient population is so small and the dose-finding work bleeds naturally into efficacy assessment.
After the trials are complete, you assemble what is called the BLA — the Biologics License Application — and you submit it to FDA.

The BLA is a colossal document, typically tens of thousands of pages, that compiles everything you have ever learned about the drug: the manufacturing process, the preclinical data, every clinical trial result, every adverse event, every analytical method.
The agency reviews the BLA, sometimes consults with an advisory committee, and either approves the drug, requests more information, or rejects it.
Approval is not the end.
There is a long tail of post-market commitments: continued safety surveillance, long-term follow-up registries, sometimes additional confirmatory trials, manufacturing monitoring.
For gene therapy in particular, the post-market commitments can extend for fifteen years after a patient is dosed, which is a longer follow-up obligation than for any small-molecule drug.
So that is the whole pipeline, in one paragraph.
Concept, preclinical, pre-IND, IND, Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3, BLA, approval, post-market.
Ten stages, each gating the next, each with its own expectations and timelines and ways of failing.
The whole journey, from the moment somebody first sketches a vector on a napkin to the moment a pharmacist hands a vial to a clinician, takes ten to fifteen years for a typical successful program.
It costs, depending on how you count, hundreds of millions to over a billion dollars.
And as I said in Segment 1, about nine out of ten programs that enter Phase 1 never get all the way to the end.
In the next segment, we are going to zoom in on the preclinical box and see what specifically a gene therapy has to demonstrate before the agency will let you dose a human being.
After that, in Segment 5, we go to the pre-IND meeting, and then in Segment 6 we file the IND itself.

By the time we hit the mid-point break after Segment 9, you will have walked the first half of the pipeline in detail and we will be ready in the second hour to look at the specific approved gene therapies that have made it all the way through.
